what: Your First Taste of Glycan Engineering 🚀
title: Get Started with glyenzy
text: Let’s dive right hands-example! ’ll start charming O-glycan ’s perfect demonstration:  Notice glycosidic bond labeled responsible enzyme? ’s beauty glycan biosynthesis – every connection story! (’ll skip rightmost peptide bond now.) ’s star glycan IUPAC-condensed notation: Time meet new best friend: find_enzyme() 👋 clever function reveals enzymes might hand building glycan: Cool, right? spot enzymes diagram, also whispers B4GALT1/2/3/4 potentially involved. (Don’t worry, ’ll unpack mystery soon!) wait, ’s ! want see happens add new enzyme mix? Let’s give ST3GAL1 chance work magic: Fascinating! 🎯 function returns glyrepr::glycan_structure() vector, real story happened: shiny new sialic acid got attached β1-3 Gal, β1-4 Gal completely ignored. ? ST3GAL1 incredibly picky – recognizes “Gal(β1-3)GalNAc(α1-” substrate. Getting hang ? Great! Let’s explore else glyenzy can .
code: glycan <- "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-6)[Gal(b1-3)]GalNAc(a1-" find_enzyme(glycan) #>  [1] "B4GALT1" "B4GALT2" "B4GALT3" "B4GALT4" "B4GALT5" "B4GALT6" "C1GALT1" #>  [8] "GCNT1"   "GCNT3"   "GCNT4" apply_enzyme(glycan, "ST3GAL1") #> <glycan_structure[1]> #> [1] Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-3)[Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 1

title: Get Started with glyenzy
text: Ever wondered glycan’s backstory? glyenzy molecular detective, ready solve three key mysteries: ? glycosyltransferases glycoside hydrolases involved? many times? often enzyme swing action? ’s timeline? order biochemical events unfold? Behind scenes, glyenzy catalogued reaction rules 105 enzymes molecular database. Want peek enzyme’s profile? Just use enzyme("MGAT3") prepare amazed biochemical details! Combined sophisticated motif-matching algorithms glymotif, can reconstruct glycan’s complete biography. ’ve already met find_enzyme() – perfect getting full cast characters. sometimes want targeted intel: have_enzyme() answers “enzyme X involved?” simple yes/count_enzyme() tells exactly many times enzyme got busy intel gold multiomics analysis – imagine linking glycan structures directly enzyme expression levels! 📊 Ready grand finale? Meet trace_biosynthesis() – function reconstructs glycan’s complete life story: get back directed igraph object – think molecular family tree! 🌳 vertex represents glycan structure, edge represents enzymatic step. one enzyme involved enzymatic step, multiple edges created two vertices. Let’s try complex N-glycan:  Note: plotting use TLC – ’re working prettier visualizations! 🎨
code: path <- trace_biosynthesis(glycan) path #> IGRAPH 74bbe32 DN-- 4 10 --  #> + attr: name (v/c), enzyme (e/c), step (e/n) #> + edges from 74bbe32 (vertex names): #> [1] GalNAc(a1-                       ->Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-                        #> [2] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-              ->Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-          #> [3] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-              ->Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-          #> [4] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-              ->Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-          #> [5] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-->Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-6)[Gal(b1-3)]GalNAc(a1- #> [6] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-->Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-6)[Gal(b1-3)]GalNAc(a1- #> [7] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-->Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-6)[Gal(b1-3)]GalNAc(a1- #> + ... omitted several edges glycan <- "GlcNAc(b1-2)Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" path <- trace_biosynthesis(glycan) plot(   path,   layout = layout_as_tree(path),   vertex.label = NA,   vertex.size = 10,   edge.arrow.size = 0.3,   margin = 0 )

title: Enzymes — enzyme
text: Glycosylation catalyzed two types enzymes: glycosyltransferases (GTs) glycoside hydrolases (GHs). Glycosyltransferases catalyze transfer sugar residue donor acceptor. Glycoside hydrolases catalyze removal sugar residue substrate. glyenzy provides data structure (glyenzy_enzyme) represent enzymes. Use enzyme() gene symbol load predefined enzyme. example, use enzyme("ST3GAL3") load enzyme ST3GAL3. Throughout package, can use enzyme()s enzyme argument, just use gene symbol directly. example, involve("Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-", "ST3GAL3") involve("Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-", enzyme("ST3GAL3")) equivalent.

title: Enzymes — enzyme
text: enzyme() list following elements: name: name enzyme, usually gene symbol. rules: list glyenzy_enzyme_rule objects. rule list following fields: acceptor: motif enzyme recognizes acceptor_alignment: alignment acceptor rejects: motifs enzyme rejects act product: product generated enzyme acceptor_idx: node index acceptor enzyme acts . GTs, node new residue attached . GHs, node removed. product_idx: node index product residue product structure. GTs, index newly added residue product. GHs, NULL (new residue added). new_residue: new residue added enzyme. GHs, NULL. new_linkage: linkage new residue. GHs, NULL. type: type enzyme, "GT" glycosyltransferase "GH" glycoside hydrolase. species: species enzyme, e.g. "human" "mouse". can see information printing enzyme object.

title: glyenzy: Deconstruction Glycosylation Biosynthesis — glyenzy-package
text: Simulates biosynthetic process glycosylation silico, enabling computational analysis glycan biosynthesis pathways. Key features include: identifying specific glycosyltransferases responsible glycosidic bond bond level; reconstructing complete biosynthetic pathways given glycans; exploring possible transformations two glycan structures via glycosyltransferases glycosidases; predicting glycans can synthesized substrate structures enzyme sets. package supports multiple glycan types including N-glycans, O-GalNAc, O-Man, O-GlcNAc, O-Fuc, O-Glc. downstream component glycoverse ecosystem, integrates seamlessly 'glyrepr', 'glyparse', 'glymotif' provide practical, analysis-ready insights glycan biosynthesis omics researchers.

title: Make a Custom Enzyme — make_enzyme
text: enzymes really picky. reject act certain glycans motifs present, even acceptor perfectly matched. example, want define enzyme adds Neu5Ac residue Gal residue Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- motif, a1-3 Fuc GlcNAc, can set "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" acceptor "Fuc(a1-3)[Gal(b1-4)]GlcNAc(b1-" rejects. can provide multiple rejects vector. example, want reject Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- either a1-3 Fuc GlcNAc a1-2 Fuc Gal, can set c("Fuc(a1-3)[Gal(b1-4)]GlcNAc(b1-", "Fuc(a1-2)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-") rejects. Note restrictions rejects: acceptor_alignment "whole", rejects set. acceptor must substructure rejects. example, a1-3 Fuc example , just set "Fuc(a1-3)GlcNAc(b1-" rejects, "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" (acceptor) substructure "Fuc(a1-3)GlcNAc(b1-" (reject). rejects structure acceptor. rejects NULL, means enzyme reject motifs.

title: Make a Custom Enzyme — make_enzyme
text: product acceptor transformed enzyme. glycosyltransferases (GTs), product exactly one residue acceptor. glycoside hydrolases (GHs), product exactly one less residue acceptor. multiple possible products, put different rules. Take example , want add Neu5Ac residue Gal residue Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- motif, can set "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" product.

title: Make a Custom Enzyme — make_enzyme
code: library(glyrepr)  # Create a custom enzyme that adds a Neu5Ac residue to the Gal residue make_enzyme(   name = "MySiaT",   rules = list(     list(       acceptor = "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-",       acceptor_alignment = "terminal",       rejects = NULL,       product = "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"     )   ),   type = "GT",   species = "human" ) #>  #> ── Enzyme: MySiaT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Type: "GT" (Glycosyltransferase) #> ℹ Species: "human" #>  #> ── Rules (1) ── #>  #> → Rule 1: terminal alignment #> Acceptor: "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> Product: "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"  # Create a custom enzyme that removes a Gal residue from a Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-` motif make_enzyme(   name = "MyGalH",   rules = list(     list(       acceptor = "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-",       acceptor_alignment = "terminal",       rejects = NULL,       product = "GlcNAc(b1-"     )   ),   type = "GH",   species = "human" ) #>  #> ── Enzyme: MyGalH ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Type: "GH" (Glycoside hydrolase) #> ℹ Species: "human" #>  #> ── Rules (1) ── #>  #> → Rule 1: terminal alignment #> Acceptor: "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> Product: "GlcNAc(b1-"  # Create a custom enzyme with rejects make_enzyme(   name = "MySiaT",   rules = list(     list(       acceptor = "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-",       acceptor_alignment = "terminal",       rejects = c(         # Reject a1-3 Fuc on GlcNAc         "Fuc(a1-3)[Gal(b1-4)]GlcNAc(b1-",         # Reject a1-2 Fuc on Gal         "Fuc(a1-2)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"       ),       product = "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"     )   ),   type = "GT",   species = "human" ) #>  #> ── Enzyme: MySiaT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Type: "GT" (Glycosyltransferase) #> ℹ Species: "human" #>  #> ── Rules (1) ── #>  #> → Rule 1: terminal alignment #> Acceptor: "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> Product: "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> Rejects: #> "Fuc(a1-3)[Gal(b1-4)]GlcNAc(b1-" #> "Fuc(a1-2)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"  # Create a custom enzyme with more than one rule make_enzyme(   name = "MySiaT",   rules = list(     list(       acceptor = "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-",       acceptor_alignment = "terminal",       rejects = NULL,       product = "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"     ),     # same acceptor, different product     list(       acceptor = "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-",       acceptor_alignment = "terminal",       rejects = NULL,       product = "Neu5Gc(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"     )   ),   type = "GT",   species = "human" ) #>  #> ── Enzyme: MySiaT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> ℹ Type: "GT" (Glycosyltransferase) #> ℹ Species: "human" #>  #> ── Rules (2) ── #>  #> → Rule 1: terminal alignment #> Acceptor: "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> Product: "Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> → Rule 2: terminal alignment #> Acceptor: "Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-" #> Product: "Neu5Gc(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"

title: Match Residues Added by an Enzyme — match_enzyme
text: algorithm takes intentionally inclusive approach, assuming possible isoenzymes capable catalyzing given reaction may involved. title: glyenzy 0.4.0
text: Strengthen validation rejects field enzyme rules: reject must contain acceptor motif. Rework rejects handling apply_enzyme() (callers) evaluate rejects per match rather per glycan, improving accuracy glycans multiple acceptor matches. Update built-rules MAN2A1, MAN2A2, MGAT3, MGAT4A, MGAT4B align new reject semantics. Remove now-redundant rejects_alignment field enzyme_rule objects; acceptor_alignment reused reject checks.
